title: Song of Solomon 6
subtitle: The Beloved Found; “I Am My Beloved’s”

heb.paragraph: "My beloved has gone down to his garden (gannîm), to the beds of spices" (v.2). The garden returns. After the crisis of chapter 5, the beloved knows where her lover is: in the garden (her). "I am my beloved’s and my beloved is mine" (v.3) — the formula from 2:16 reversed. The shift from "mine/his" to "his/mine" marks growth: from claiming to belonging.

refs.note: "My beloved is mine and I am his" — the earlier formula. The reversal marks growth.
"I no longer live, but Christ lives in me" — the surrender-first identity that echoes the reversed formula.

notes.note: "Where has your beloved gone? Which way did your beloved turn?" The Daughters respond to the wasf with desire to find him too. Her description created longing in others.
"My beloved has gone down to his garden... I am my beloved’s and my beloved is mine." She knows where he is. The crisis has resolved into deeper certainty.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Alter: the formula’s reversal is the poem’s most subtle character development. Two words rearranged; the entire orientation shifts from possession to devotion. Alter's literary translation draws attention to the poetic artistry of the Hebrew — rhythm, parallelism, sound play, and imagery that constitute not merely decoration but meaning. The form of the poem enacts its content: how the words are arranged is inseparable from what the words say.

Calvin: "I am my beloved’s" — belonging precedes possessing. Calvin: the soul that first surrenders to Christ then receives Christ. The order matters. Calvin's reading characteristically emphasizes divine sovereignty and human responsibility in tension — God ordains the outcome while the actors remain morally accountable. This dual emphasis reflects Calvin's broader hermeneutic: Scripture always speaks both about God's plan and about human duty under that plan.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET: "gone down to his garden" may have erotic overtones: the garden is the beloved herself (4:12–16). He is with her.
The NET: the formula shift from 2:16 to 6:3 is widely noted by commentators as evidence of relational development within the poem.

longman.source: Tremper Longman III, Song of Songs, NICOT (2001) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Longman: the reversal of the formula (from 2:16 "mine/his" to 6:3 "his/mine") tracks the relationship’s maturation. Early love claims; mature love surrenders. The progression will complete in 7:10: "I belong to my beloved, and his desire is for me.". This observation invites further reflection on the passage's contribution to the book's theological argument. The text operates on multiple levels simultaneously — historical, literary, and theological — and each level enriches the others when the passage is read with attention to its canonical context.

garrett.source: Duane A. Garrett, Song of Songs / Proverbs, NAC (1993) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Garrett: the resolution is anticlimactic by design. After the drama of ch 5, the answer is simple: he is in his garden. Love’s crises often resolve quietly. This observation invites further reflection on the passage's contribution to the book's theological argument. The text operates on multiple levels simultaneously — historical, literary, and theological — and each level enriches the others when the passage is read with attention to its canonical context.

hist.context: The Daughters ask where the beloved has gone (v.1). She answers: he has gone to his garden. The crisis is resolved not through dramatic reunion but through quiet knowledge: she knows where he is, and she knows she belongs to him. The mutual-belonging formula evolves: "I am my beloved’s" comes first now. She leads with surrender, not possession.

sections.header: Verses 4–13 — “Beautiful as Tirzah, Lovely as Jerusalem”

heb.paragraph: "You are as beautiful as Tirzah, my darling, lovely as Jerusalem, majestic as troops with banners" (v.4). Tirzah means "beautiful" and was the early capital of the northern kingdom. The beloved is compared to two capital cities — the beauty of both kingdoms combined. And then: "majestic as troops with banners" — her beauty has military force.

refs.note: "A wife of noble character is her husband’s crown" — the same theology of marital honour.
"The Holy City, the new Jerusalem, prepared as a bride beautifully dressed" — Jerusalem as bride, echoing the Song’s city-as-beauty imagery.

notes.note: "Beautiful as Tirzah, lovely as Jerusalem, majestic as troops with banners. Turn your eyes from me; they overwhelm me." Her beauty is overpowering. Even the lover needs relief from its intensity.

"Sixty queens, eighty concubines, and virgins beyond number. But my dove, my perfect one, is unique, the only daughter of her mother, the favourite of the one who bore her." Amidst infinite options, she is singular.
"Who is this that appears like the dawn, fair as the moon, bright as the sun?" She is described in cosmic terms: dawn, moon, sun. The beloved is not just humanly beautiful but celestially radiant.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Alter: Tirzah and Jerusalem — two capitals, two kingdoms, two forms of beauty unified in one beloved. The poem is implicitly political: love unites what politics divided.
Alter: the celestial progression (dawn → moon → sun → stars) creates an ascending scale of luminosity. She becomes brighter with each comparison.
Calvin: "unique" among queens and concubines — Calvin: the church is Christ’s unique bride. Many religions exist; only one is the true beloved.
Calvin: the dawn/moon/sun imagery — the church grows in glory as it grows in Christ. "The path of the righteous is like the morning sun, shining ever brighter" (Prov 4:18).

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET: Tirzah was the northern capital before Samaria. Its name means "pleasant/beautiful" — the comparison works on both levels: city and etymology.
The NET: the celestial comparisons use three ascending luminaries: dawn (first light), moon (night’s brightest), sun (full radiance). Each is more brilliant than the last.

longman.source: Tremper Longman III, Song of Songs, NICOT (2001) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Longman: the comparison with queens and concubines makes exclusivity the point. Not "you are the most beautiful" (comparative) but "you are the only one" (absolute). Love singles out.
Longman: dawn/moon/sun progression moves from dim light to full brightness. The beloved’s beauty increases as the lover describes it, like the sun rising.

garrett.source: Duane A. Garrett, Song of Songs / Proverbs, NAC (1993) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Garrett: Tirzah as a beauty-comparison suggests the poem predates the fall of the northern kingdom (722 BC) or uses archaic traditions. The geographical references ground the poetry in real landscape.
Garrett: "majestic as the stars in procession" — the astronomical imagery elevates the beloved beyond earthly comparison. She is cosmic.

hist.context: The Lover praises the Beloved again: beautiful as Tirzah, lovely as Jerusalem (v.4). He asks her to turn her eyes away because they overwhelm him (v.5). The wasf elements repeat from chapter 4 (hair, teeth, temples). Then a comparison: sixty queens, eighty concubines, virgins without number — but she is unique, the only one, her mother’s favourite (vv.8–9). The Daughters see her and call her blessed (v.9). "Who is this that appears like the dawn, fair as the moon, bright as the sun, majestic as the stars?" (v.10).

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVYour teeth are like a flock of sheep coming up from the washing. Each has its twin, not one of them is missing.ESVYour teeth are like a flock of ewes that have come up from the washing; all of them bear twins; not one among them has lost its young.NIVTurn your eyes from me; they overwhelm me. Your hair is like a flock of goats descending from Gilead.ESVTurn away your eyes from me, for they overwhelm me— Your hair is like a flock of goats leaping down the slopes of Gilead.

rec.text: ""Where has your beloved gone? Which way did your beloved turn?" The Daughters respond to the wasf with desire to find him too. Her description created longing in others."

rows.label: Cosmic Beauty — 6:4–13
rows.text: Beautiful as Tirzah, fair as the moon, bright as the sun. "My perfect one is unique."

hebtext.note: "My beloved has gone down to his garden (gannîm), to the beds of spices" (v.2). The garden returns. After the crisis of chapter 5, the beloved knows where her lover is: in the garden (her). "I am my beloved’s and my beloved is mine" (v.3) — the formula from 2:16 reversed. The shift from "mine/his" to "his/mine" marks growth: from claiming to belonging.

"You are as beautiful as Tirzah, my darling, lovely as Jerusalem, majestic as troops with banners" (v.4). Tirzah means "beautiful" and was the early capital of the northern kingdom. The beloved is compared to two capital cities — the beauty of both kingdoms combined. And then: "majestic as troops with banners" — her beauty has military force.

themes.note: Covenant peaks: the reversed formula marks relational maturity. Holiness: cosmic beauty language. Prophecy: Jerusalem-as-bride anticipates Revelation 21.
